---
format:
  revealjs:
    code-line-numbers: false
    theme: [default, workshop_data_availability.scss]
    slide-number: true
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    footer: "DSPG 2026 | Virginia Tech"
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    embed-resources: true
    incremental: false
---

## {.title-custom data-menu-title="Data Availability Workshop"}

::: {.title-block}
::: {.t-title}
Organizing and Documenting Data Availability
:::
::: {.t-rule}
:::
::: {.t-authors}
**Instructor:** Pragati Dahal
:::
::: {.t-program}
Data Science for the Public Good · Summer 2026
:::
::: {.t-logos}
![](images/kohlcentre_logo.png){height="70"}
:::
:::

---

## What Are We Building Today?

::: {.scope-core}
::: {.mini-heading}
By the end of this workshop, you will be able to:
:::
- **Identify** which variables exist in your dataset across years
- **Audit** data availability programmatically in R
- **Document** your findings in a structured, shareable format
- **Visualize** the results as an interactive HTML dashboard
:::

::: {.note}
Before we start — open the finished dashboard in your browser. That is where we are going.
:::

---

## Where This Fits in Your Project

::: {.scope-core}
::: {.mini-heading}
The problem
:::
Before you clean your data, you need to know what you actually have.
If a variable only exists for 7 out of 14 years — you need to know that *before* you spend hours cleaning it.
:::

::: {.scope-conditional}
::: {.mini-heading}
Today's step
:::
**Raw Data → [YOU ARE HERE] → Cleaning → Analysis → Report**

The step between downloading data and cleaning it is usually skipped. Today we make it explicit.
:::

---

## {background-color="#861F41" data-menu-title="Demo Only"}

::: {style="color: white; text-align: center; padding-top: 160px;"}
::: {style="font-size: 0.7em; letter-spacing: 0.2em; text-transform: uppercase; opacity: 0.7; margin-bottom: 16px;"}
Instructor Demo
:::
::: {style="font-size: 2em; font-weight: 800; font-family: Georgia, serif;"}
How the Data Was Downloaded
:::
::: {style="font-size: 0.9em; opacity: 0.75; margin-top: 16px;"}
You can try to use API for today's workshop, there is a downloaded file that we can use — but try to understand what the API did.
:::
:::

---

## How ACS Data Is Pulled with tidycensus

::: {.scope-core}
::: {.mini-heading}
This is how acs_virginia_raw.csv was created
:::
`tidycensus` sends a request to the Census API and returns a clean dataframe.
One function call, one year, one variable:
:::

```r
library(tidycensus)
library(tidyverse)

get_acs(
  geography = "county",      # unit of analysis
  variables = "B19013_001",  # ACS variable code
  state     = "VA",          # Virginia counties only
  year      = 2022,          # which year
  survey    = "acs5"         # 5-year estimates
)
```

::: {.note}
Output: GEOID, NAME, variable, estimate, moe — exactly what is in acs_virginia_raw.csv
:::

---

## Downloading Multiple Variables Across Years

::: {.scope-core}
::: {.mini-heading}
Pull each variable separately — loop over years
:::
This is what produced `acs_virginia_raw.csv`.
:::

```r
vars  <- c("B19013_001","B17001_002","B15003_022",
           "B27001_001","B01003_001","B28002_004")
years <- 2010:2023

raw_data <- map_dfr(vars, function(var) {
  map_dfr(years, function(yr) {
    tryCatch({
      get_acs(geography="county", variables=var,
              state="VA", year=yr, survey="acs5") |>
        mutate(year = yr)
    }, error = function(e) NULL)
  })
})

write_csv(raw_data, "acs_virginia_raw.csv")
```

::: {.note}
This takes several minutes and makes ~84 API calls. We ran it once and saved the result.
That is the file you are working from today.
:::

---

## {background-color="#E5751F" data-menu-title="Hands On"}

::: {style="color: white; text-align: center; padding-top: 160px;"}
::: {style="font-size: 0.7em; letter-spacing: 0.2em; text-transform: uppercase; opacity: 0.8; margin-bottom: 16px;"}
Hands On
:::
::: {style="font-size: 2em; font-weight: 800; font-family: Georgia, serif;"}
Now We Work From the CSV
:::
::: {style="font-size: 0.9em; opacity: 0.85; margin-top: 16px;"}
No API needed — the data is already downloaded for you.
:::
:::

---

## What's in acs_virginia_raw.csv

::: {.scope-core}
::: {.mini-heading}
Load and explore the data
:::
:::

```r
library(tidyverse)

raw <- read_csv("acs_virginia_raw.csv")
glimpse(raw)
```

::: {.scope-conditional}
::: {.mini-heading}
What you will see
:::
- **9,725 rows** — one per county × variable × year combination
- **6 columns:** `GEOID`, `NAME`, `variable`, `estimate`, `moe`, `year`
- Not all variables appear in all years — that is what we are about to audit
:::

---

## Auditing Availability from the CSV

::: {.scope-core}
::: {.mini-heading}
Which variables exist in which years?
:::
Instead of making API calls, we check the CSV directly.
`distinct()` gives us every variable-year combination that actually exists.
We compare against the full 2010–2023 range to find the gaps.
:::

```r
years_in_data <- raw |>
  distinct(variable, year)

all_combos <- expand_grid(
  variable = unique(raw$variable),
  year     = 2010:2023
)

audit_results <- all_combos |>
  left_join(years_in_data |> mutate(available = TRUE),
            by = c("variable", "year")) |>
  mutate(available = replace_na(available, FALSE))
```

---

## Adding Labels and Categories

```r
var_metadata <- tibble(
  variable = c("B19013_001","B17001_002","B15003_022",
               "B27001_001","B01003_001","B28002_004"),
  label    = c("Median Household Income","Population Below Poverty",
               "Bachelor's Degree","Health Insurance Universe",
               "Total Population","Broadband Access"),
  category = c("Socioeconomic","Socioeconomic","Socioeconomic",
               "Healthcare Access","Demographics","Infrastructure")
)

audit_final <- audit_results |>
  left_join(var_metadata, by = "variable") |>
  group_by(variable, label, category) |>
  summarise(
    n_years       = sum(available),
    years_present = list(year[available == TRUE]),
    missing_years = list(year[available == FALSE]),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Save so the dashboard QMD and Shiny app can load it
saveRDS(audit_final, "audit_final.rds")
```

---

## Building the Dashboard

::: {.contrib-grid}
::: {.contrib-column}
::: {.contrib-heading}
Option 1 — reactable widget
:::
```r
library(reactable)
library(htmlwidgets)

widget <- reactable(
  audit_final,
  filterable = TRUE,
  searchable = TRUE
)

saveWidget(widget,
  "data_availability.html")
```
Quick, functional, shareable. No AI needed. Works offline as a standalone HTML file.
:::

::: {.contrib-column}
::: {.contrib-heading}
Option 2 — Quarto Dashboard
:::
First run the exercise script to create `audit_final.rds`, then:

```r
quarto::quarto_render(
  "data_availability_dashboard.qmd"
)
```

Styled interactive table using `reactable` inside a Quarto HTML document. Open `data_availability_dashboard.html` in your browser.
:::
:::

---

## Building the Dashboard

::: {.contrib-grid}
::: {.contrib-column}
::: {.contrib-heading}
Option 3 — Shiny App
:::
Make sure `audit_final.rds` exists, then:

```r
shiny::runApp(
  "data_availability_dashboard_shinyapp.R"
)
```

Live reactive filtering — filters update instantly without re-rendering. Runs in RStudio. Good Shiny practice.

::: {.note}
Shiny requires R to be running. Unlike Options 1 and 2, it cannot be shared as a standalone file.
:::
:::

::: {.contrib-column}
::: {.contrib-heading}
Option 4 — R + Claude
:::
```r
library(jsonlite)

cat(toJSON(audit_final |>
  mutate(
    years_present =
      map(years_present, as.integer),
    missing_years =
      map(missing_years, as.integer)
  ), pretty = TRUE))
```
Copy JSON → paste into Claude with the prompt on the next slide → polished color-coded HTML dashboard.
:::
:::

---

## The Claude Prompt

```markdown
Create a self-contained interactive HTML dashboard for an ACS variable availability audit.

STYLE
- Virginia Tech colors: maroon #861F41, orange #E5751F, cream background
- Fonts: Cormorant Garamond (headings), JetBrains Mono (code), DM Sans (body)

LAYOUT
- Dark maroon hero header with grid texture, italic serif title, monospace metadata
- White stat bar: total variables, full/partial counts, animated fill-rate progress bar
- Filter pills: coverage (All / Full / Partial) and category — each category in its own color
- Grid: left column shows variable name, ACS code, category badge, mini progress bar
  Year columns show filled colored squares (available) or dashed grey squares (missing)
- Tooltip on hover: variable name, year status (✓/✗), coverage fraction, missing years as chips
- Footer with institution name and data source

RULES
- Vanilla JS only, no libraries
- Each category gets a distinct color across cells, badges, pills, and progress bars
- Tooltip repositions to stay in viewport
- Filters show empty state when nothing matches
- Fill-rate bar animates on load

DATA [Paste JSON here]
```

::: {.note}
The more specific your prompt, the better the output. Mention colors, layout, interactivity.
:::

---

## Apply This to Your Own Project

::: {.question-grid}
::: {.question-card}
::: {.question-title}
Step 1 — Read your proposal
:::
What variables do you need? Write them down before touching any code.
:::
::: {.question-card}
::: {.question-title}
Step 2 — Find the codes
:::
Use `load_variables()` to find the ACS table and cell for each variable you need.
:::
::: {.question-card}
::: {.question-title}
Step 3 — Run the audit
:::
Which years are actually available? Don't assume — check programmatically.
:::
::: {.question-card}
::: {.question-title}
Step 4 — Document
:::
Add your own labels and categories. Save the audit script — this is part of your project documentation.
:::
:::

---

## Summary

::: {.scope-core}
::: {.mini-heading}
What you learned today
:::
- How ACS data is pulled using `tidycensus` and why variables have different year coverage
- How to audit data availability from a CSV using `distinct()` and `left_join()`
- How to document findings with human-readable labels and categories
- How to turn your audit into a shareable HTML dashboard — four ways
:::

::: {.scope-conditional}
::: {.mini-heading}
The pipeline
:::
`acs_virginia_raw.csv` → `audit_results` → `audit_final` → reactable / QMD / Shiny / Claude → HTML
:::

---

## Questions? {.title-custom}

::: {.title-block}
::: {.t-title}
Thank you
:::
::: {.t-rule}
:::
::: {.t-authors}
**Pragati Dahal** <br>
Graduate Research Assistant · Agricultural & Applied Economics <br>
Virginia Tech <br>
pdahal23@vt.edu
:::
::: {.t-program}
Workshop materials available in the shared DSPG folder
:::
::: {.t-logos}
![](images/kohlcentre_logo.png){height="70"}
:::
:::
